title: Ohm's Law Calculator
description: Industrial-grade calculator for electrical power and resistance analysis, compliant with IEC 60038, ISO 50001, and Lean Six Sigma standards.

inputs.label: Voltage (V)
inputs.businessContext: Nominal voltage per IEC 60038. Typical industrial values: 230V single-phase, 400V three-phase.

inputs.label: Current (I)
inputs.businessContext: Load current in amperes. Used for conductor sizing and protection coordination.

inputs.label: Resistance (R)
inputs.businessContext: DC resistance or AC impedance at power frequency. Includes conductor, contact, and load resistance.

inputs.label: Power Factor (cos φ)
inputs.businessContext: Ratio of real power to apparent power. Typical industrial loads: 0.8-1.0. Used for AC power calculations.

inputs.label: Phase Type
inputs.businessContext: Single-phase or three-phase system. Affects power calculation formulas per IEC 60364.

inputs.label: Ambient Temperature
inputs.businessContext: Ambient temperature for resistance temperature correction. Copper conductor TCR = 0.00393 /°C.

rules.message: At least two of Voltage, Current, or Resistance must be provided. If all three are given, they must satisfy Ohm's Law within 5% tolerance.
Voltage, Current, and Resistance do not satisfy Ohm's Law within 5% tolerance. Check measurement accuracy.
Three-phase systems typically have power factor >= 0.6. Verify measurement.

thresholds.message: Current exceeds 100 A. Verify conductor ampacity per NEC Table 310.15(B)(16).
Voltage exceeds 1000 V. High-voltage safety procedures required per NFPA 70E.
Power factor below 0.7 indicates poor efficiency. Consider power factor correction capacitors.
Ambient temperature above 60°C may require derating of conductors per IEC 60364-5-52.

formulas.name: Real Power (DC or Resistive AC)
formulas.description: P = V * I (for DC or unity power factor)

formulas.name: Real Power (AC with power factor)
formulas.description: P = V * I * cos φ (single-phase) or P = √3 * V * I * cos φ (three-phase)

formulas.name: Apparent Power
formulas.description: S = V * I (single-phase) or S = √3 * V * I (three-phase)

formulas.name: Temperature Corrected Resistance
formulas.description: R_t = R_ref * (1 + α * (T - T_ref)), α = 0.00393 /°C for copper, T_ref = 20°C

primary.label: Real Power (P)
primary.description: Calculated real power consumption or generation.

reactivePower.label: Reactive Power (Q)
reactivePower.description: Non-working power due to inductive or capacitive loads.

powerLoss.label: I²R Loss (P_loss)
powerLoss.description: Power dissipated as heat in conductors and windings.

correctedResistance.label: Temperature Corrected Resistance
correctedResistance.description: Resistance adjusted for ambient temperature (copper TCR).

powerFactorActual.label: Actual Power Factor
powerFactorActual.description: Calculated power factor from real and apparent power.

hiddenLossDrivers.label: High Current
hiddenLossDrivers.description: I²R losses increase quadratically with current. Consider reducing load or increasing conductor size.

hiddenLossDrivers.label: Low Power Factor
hiddenLossDrivers.description: Low power factor increases apparent power and line losses. Power factor correction can reduce losses by up to 20%.

hiddenLossDrivers.label: High Ambient Temperature
hiddenLossDrivers.description: Increased resistance due to temperature raises I²R losses. For every 10°C above 20°C, copper resistance increases by ~3.9%.

hiddenLossDrivers.label: Harmonic Distortion
hiddenLossDrivers.description: Non-linear loads cause harmonic currents that increase RMS current and additional losses in transformers and conductors.

suggestedActions.action: Reduce load current or increase conductor cross-section to lower I²R losses and prevent overheating.
Install power factor correction capacitors to improve power factor above 0.95, reducing apparent power and utility penalties.
Improve ventilation or relocate equipment to cooler area. Consider using higher temperature rated insulation (e.g., 90°C rated cable).
Check voltage regulation at point of common coupling. Voltage deviation >10% may cause equipment malfunction and increased losses.
Perform harmonic analysis. Install passive or active harmonic filters to reduce THD and improve system efficiency.
